chapters.title: Chapter 1: The Map of Silence
chapters.text:
The anomaly map occupied the full width of Lian's primary display for eleven days before anyone decided what to do about it.

It was not that no one noticed. The anomaly was the first thing Lian reported at every daily briefing; the shaded territories — the 60% of the substrate that the restoration had not reached, where the entity's signal was moving toward a pre-Archive pattern — were the background condition that every briefing opened with and returned to. Everyone noticed. The decision of what to do about it was the thing that required eleven days.

The restoration liaison position was six weeks old. Corin had spent those six weeks translating the oversight committee's queries into responses that said what the restoration team could actually report, managing the archive inventory of Aldric Draven's fifteen years of research, and sitting in briefings where the anomaly map was displayed and discussed and not acted on. The not-acting-on had its own logic — the anomaly was not an emergency, not a crisis, not a signal that required immediate response. It was a thing that was happening in territories the restoration had not reached, and the restoration had not reached those territories because the restoration was at 14.3% and the unrestored territories were fifty years away at current rate.

The not-acting-on had its logic. The logic had held for eleven days. On the twelfth day, Corin read the second thread's abandonment note again — *the investigation requires institutional resources that I cannot maintain from the current position* — and went to Iris.

"I need to go there," he said.

Iris looked at the anomaly map on the data pad he was carrying. She had been filing the monitoring reports in the registry for eleven days, adding them to the documentation in the specific order she added everything — date, context, what was known and what was not. She had been filing them with the quality of someone who understood that the specific thing she was documenting was going to require someone to go look at it directly, and that the someone was going to be the person carrying the data pad.

"Yes," she said. "You do."

---

The field expedition proposal was three pages. Corin drafted it in the liaison office between 2100 and 2340, the night after the conversation with Iris. The proposal specified: the expedition's purpose (direct field monitoring of the substrate anomaly in unrestored territories, cross-referenced against Aldric Draven archive second thread documentation); the expedition's composition (Corin Draven, liaison; Lian Asher, monitoring; one monitoring team member for equipment operation; Iris Vane, documentation); the expedition's route (eastern approach, through the borderlands, into the eastern communities); the expedition's timeline (twelve days, six days outbound, four days on-site, two days return); the expedition's monitoring equipment (portable substrate array, three units, designed for field deployment without fixed infrastructure).

He submitted it through the liaison position's official channel at 0023. He sent a copy to Hollan. He sent a copy to Elara Venn, who was still in contact with the liaison office through the twice-weekly relay she had established after the censure.

At 0815 the following morning, Hollan came to the office.

"The oversight committee will want a security attachment," Hollan said. "The borderlands are not monitored. The eastern communities have had no Archive presence since the field calibration thirty-one years ago." He paused. "The people in those communities will know what an Archive monitoring team looks like. I need you to understand that the reaction to your arrival may not be what the institutional framing of the expedition suggests."

"I know," Corin said.

"The 216," Hollan said. "Some of them are in those communities. The people who were permanently harmed by the last time an Archive operation reached their territory." He paused. "I'm not saying don't go. I'm saying go understanding what the expedition means to the people who are going to watch you arrive."

"That's what Iris is for," Corin said. "The registry. She documented the 216. She's coming to show the registry to the people who have been carrying those names."

Hollan was quiet for a moment. That was not the answer he had expected from the liaison position, which was a position he had helped create on the assumption that it would translate between the institution and the restoration operation and not between the institution and the communities the institution had permanently harmed thirty-one years ago. The answer suggested the liaison position's operating scope was larger than the job description.

He approved the security attachment — two people, not a full team, dressed without Archive insignia — and signed the expedition authorization by noon.

---

Iris spent the evening before departure reviewing the registry.

She did this at her monitoring hub, the same desk she had been working at for twenty-three years, the same desk where the registry had grown from a handwritten notebook to a multi-terminal archive that now contained, in the column she had added three weeks before Joren's arrival, the 216 names that the Null Congregation had been carrying in memory since the founding. The 216 names now had entries in the registry in the same format as every other entry: date of harm, category of harm, status. The category for the 216 was a new category — Iris had created it when she added the entries. She had not used the Archive's existing harm-category vocabulary because the Archive's existing vocabulary did not have a category for permanently stripped Resonance architecture. She had named the category what it was: Resonance Architecture Removal (Field Calibration, Eastern Zone, Year 31 Pre-Registry).

She reviewed the 216 entries. She reviewed the names against the names Joren had recited for her when he visited — the names the Congregation carried, the names Vessa had memorized at age five. All 216 present. All 216 accounted for in the document. All 216 outside every restoration architecture that had ever existed, including the architecture that had just been completed at 61.8% coverage.

She looked at the expedition route on the planning document Corin had shared with her. The eastern communities were on the route. Some of the 216 lived there.

She printed the 216 entries on the registry's document function — the physical output, the paper, the thing she had been producing for twenty-three years for the people whose names were in it. She printed two copies. One for the registry's physical archive. One to carry east.

She put the registry copy in her pack. She packed the same things she had packed for every field operation she had done in twenty-three years: documentation materials, a spare data pad, three days of shelf-stable food in case the expedition timeline slipped. She was ready by 2200.

---

Lian selected the portable substrate array equipment from the monitoring team's field storage in three hours.

The equipment had been built for monitoring work in territories without Archive infrastructure. The array units were the size of a large data pad, with collapsible signal receivers that extended to half a meter. They had been prototyped by Lian's team in the weeks after the deployment, as a response to the realization that the restoration's operational territory was about to expand into areas where fixed monitoring was not possible.

"The equipment will read the substrate signal at the same scale as the fixed monitoring arrays," Lian said. "But it will also read something the fixed arrays can't — the localized variation. The way the signal changes across fifty meters instead of fifty kilometers." She pulled the first unit from its case. "The anomaly on the main display is a macro pattern. What the field equipment will show us is the micro pattern." She looked at the unit in her hands. "The entity's substrate signal in the unrestored territories is moving toward a pre-Archive pattern. That's what the fixed arrays tell us. What the fixed arrays can't tell us is why there — why the specific locations — and what the signal is doing at the scale of the terrain."

"What does the second thread's documentation say about that?" Corin asked.

"Your father monitored the uncontacted territories with field equipment he built himself from pre-collapse components. The documentation has seventeen pages of field notes — three years of them, before the 17-X classification stopped his access." She set the unit back in the case. "The field notes describe the entity's signal in specific terrain — the badlands near the eastern communities, the northern transition zones, three locations in the border territory. He was mapping the variation. He found something consistent in one location that he couldn't find in the others." She paused. "He never finished the map. The classification stopped him before he could."

"We're going to finish the map," Corin said.

Lian looked at the equipment case. "Yes."

---

Cross came to the office on the afternoon before departure.

He opened the door and sat in the visitor chair as if the visitor chair was where he belonged — which, in the six weeks of the liaison position's existence, it had become. Cross was the only Council-adjacent figure who came to the office without scheduling through the official channel. Everyone else used the channel. Cross knocked or came in.

"The second thread," he said. "Lian briefed me."

"You read the inventory," Corin said.

"I reverse-engineered the frequency maps from your father's research twelve years ago," Cross said. "I know every part of that research that was in the 17-X classified archive. The second thread wasn't in the classified archive. It was in the unclassified personal notes — the part the classification didn't touch because it wasn't technically part of the program." He paused. "I didn't know the second thread existed until the declassification opened the personal archive."

"What does it tell you?"

Cross looked at the anomaly map on Corin's desk terminal. "It tells me that your father spent three years watching the entity's signal in territories where no one else was watching. And that the signal in those territories was doing something he didn't have the resources to characterize." He paused. "Lian's anomaly is in the same territory. The same terrain your father was mapping when the classification stopped him."

"I know."

Cross was quiet for a moment. He had the expression of someone who has spent twelve years carrying a specific set of technical decisions and has recently received information about what those decisions were adjacent to. Not the decisions themselves. What was beside them.

"I want to come," he said.

Corin looked at the expedition proposal — the five-person composition, the twelve-day timeline, the route. Cross was not on it. Cross was the Archive's external affairs director, and the borderlands were the Archive's external affairs territory, and bringing the external affairs director to a community that the Archive's external affairs operation had contracted to suppress for eleven years was a specific kind of complicated.

"No," Corin said.

Cross looked at him.

"Not this trip," Corin said. "If what Lian finds confirms the second thread's direction — if the entity's signal in those territories is doing what the three years of monitoring was trying to characterize — then we'll need the technical background you can bring to the second expedition. But the first contact in the eastern communities is not the time to bring the Archive's external affairs director."

Cross sat with that for a moment. He had expected the answer. Expecting it and receiving it were different.

"All right," he said.

He left without arguing. That was also not what Corin had expected.

---

Departure was at 0600 on a Tuesday, before the Citadel's administrative day began.

The expedition team was five: Corin, Lian, a monitoring technician from Lian's team named Pell, Iris, and — after Hollan's security attachment was negotiated down — one person from the Citadel's outer perimeter security staff who had grown up in the eastern communities before joining the Archive's contractor network and who had specifically requested to come when the expedition was posted on the staff relay board.

Her name was Fay. She was twenty-nine. She had left the eastern communities at seventeen for the Archive's contractor training program and had been in the Citadel's perimeter security since. She had six people she was related to in the eastern communities. She had not been back in twelve years.

She drove the expedition vehicle — a borderlands-rated transport, no Archive insignia — out through the outer perimeter gate at 0614. The monitoring display logged them out. The credential readers logged their exit. The vehicle left the perimeter and the monitoring coverage thinned and then stopped as they passed the outer boundary of the Reckoner network's standard field.

The monitoring was gone. The substrate was still there.

Lian deployed the first portable array unit at 0652, fifteen kilometers past the monitoring boundary. She mounted it on the vehicle's roof and let it run as they drove. The unit's output appeared on the data pad in her lap.

"The signal is present at the boundary," she said. "Weaker than the anomaly map showed — the fixed arrays amplify the signal for the main display. At baseline field strength — still present. Definitely present." She looked at the data pad. "The direction: consistent with the anomaly map. Moving toward the pre-Archive pattern." She paused. "But at this scale — the localized scale — there's something else. A structure in the frequency variation that the fixed arrays were too coarse to resolve."

"What kind of structure?" Corin asked.

She was quiet for a moment. The data pad's display showed a graph of frequency against time. The graph had a pattern. Not random. Structured.

"Rhythmic," she said. "The variation is rhythmic." She paused. "The rate matches the entity's substrate response at 1947. At the moment the 47.2-omega was received as healing."

The vehicle drove east. The monitoring was behind them. The substrate was ahead.

Fay was watching the terrain through the windshield. She had been watching it since they passed the monitoring boundary, with the specific quality of someone recognizing landscape they had not seen for twelve years — the particular slope of the eastern badlands, the pre-collapse road markers that the territory used instead of the Archive's standard route signage, the water management infrastructure that the eastern communities had built from salvage in the years after the field calibration. The water infrastructure was new since she had left. She had heard about it from her relatives' relay messages. She had not seen it.

"I didn't know it was this developed," she said. The only words she had spoken since departure.

"Sixty kilometers," Lian said. "We're sixty kilometers from the Citadel's monitoring boundary and there's infrastructure I don't have on any Archive map." She paused. "The maps were built from monitoring data. The monitoring didn't reach here."

"The entity is doing something specific," Lian said. "At a specific rate. In a specific territory." She looked at the data pad. "What, and why here — that's what we're going to find out."

chapters.title: Chapter 2: The Decommission
chapters.text:
The Section 7 cooperative facility was not marked on the Archive's current operational maps. The oversight committee's revised territory registry — the one produced in the first two weeks after the censure, which had taken the 17-X classified operational footprint and converted it into a document any Archive staff member could request — listed the facility by its administrative designation: N7-Coop-14, northern transitional zone, operational years 22 through 47 post-Registry. The revised registry noted: "Voided per oversight motion 7-A. Decommission in progress."

Corin had read the designation three times before departure. He had not formed a picture from it. The picture required arriving.

The facility was a pre-collapse building — concrete and structural polymer, the kind of construction that had been standard in the decades before the substrate crisis had redirected every material allocation toward the Archive's stabilization infrastructure. It was a large building for the transitional zone. Three stories. A loading bay on the north face with a retractable metal door, currently open, showing stacked equipment crates inside. A parking area with three vehicles of the same borderlands-rated type as the expedition's transport. The facility's exterior walls had been painted once, probably in year 22, and had not been painted since. The Archive's administrative insignia — a stylized A in a circle — was still visible above the main entrance, but someone had already removed the metal placard that would have contained the facility's official name.

The placard's shadow was still on the concrete. A clean rectangle where the sun hadn't reached in twenty-five years.

Fay slowed the vehicle as they passed the entrance. She had not been asked to stop. She slowed because the facility was there and because slowing was the honest response to what the facility was.

"Section 7," Lian said. She was looking at the portable array unit's output. She had been watching the screen, not the road, since they left the overnight camp at 0620. The screen showed the substrate signal continuing its rhythmic variation, stronger as they had driven east through the morning. "The cooperative structure — this was the commercial contracting side of the monitoring operation. Not the classified program. The part that was supposed to look like resource extraction permits and infrastructure support."

"It looked like resource extraction permits and infrastructure support," Corin said.

"Yes," Lian said. "Because it was. The cooperative structure was both things simultaneously. The contracting operation generated revenue. The monitoring operation used the contracting infrastructure as cover." She paused. "That's in the oversight motion's findings. Section 4.2."

Fay had slowed further. The vehicle was nearly stopped.

"We can stop," Corin said.

Fay pulled into the parking area. She did not say anything. She parked between two of the borderlands vehicles and got out. Corin followed. Lian stayed in the vehicle with the monitoring equipment, watching the screen.

Pell got out and stood next to the vehicle. He was looking at the loading bay, the crates, the open retractable door. His expression was the expression of someone cataloguing.

Iris was already out of the vehicle and walking toward the entrance. She had her documentation pad open. She was noting the facility's physical characteristics — the concrete, the structural condition, the loading bay dimensions — in the same systematic way she documented everything. The facility was not in her expedition documentation scope. She was documenting it because the documentation habit was stronger than the scope, and because the documentation might be useful to someone.

The facility's loading bay smell was the specific smell of archived equipment — pre-collapse metal, polymer seals, the slight chemical signature of monitoring hardware that had been running for decades in a sealed space. Corin recognized it from the Citadel's lower storage levels, where equipment from the managed restoration zone was staged. The same smell. The same era of equipment. The same institutional footprint, in this building and in the Citadel's storage, coming from the same program.

---

The administrative staff inside the facility were working through the equipment inventory with the specific quality of people performing a task that has a documented procedure and a deadline. There were four of them visible from the loading bay entrance — two moving crates, one at a data terminal logging equipment codes, one carrying a clipboard with a printed checklist. They were dressed in the Archive's standard administrative contractor uniform, which did not have insignia but was recognizable by its color and cut.

The one with the clipboard looked up when the expedition team entered through the loading bay.

She was in her forties, with the alert stillness of someone who had been briefed that the decommission process might include site visits from various oversight-adjacent parties. She had not been briefed on who specifically.

"This is a controlled decommission site," she said. It was the procedural opening, not a challenge.

"Corin Draven," Corin said. "Archive restoration liaison. We're on a field expedition through the northern transitional zone. We're not here on decommission business. We stopped because the facility was on our route."

The woman with the clipboard looked at him. She did not look at the clipboard.

"Draven," she said.

"Yes."

"You're in the testimony record." She said it the way someone says something they have been thinking since they looked up from the clipboard. "The decommission package — the oversight motion's full documentation — we were given the testimony record as background material. So we understood what we were decommissioning." She paused. "Your testimony is in it. The section about the classified program."

"Yes," Corin said.

She was quiet for a moment. This was not hostility. It was the specific quality of recognition — not of a face, but of a name in a document that had changed the meaning of a job she had been doing for eleven years.

"We have about sixty percent of the equipment inventoried," she said finally. "The data terminals are the last to go. We're supposed to complete the physical inventory before the data archive is transferred to the oversight committee's designated repository." She looked at the loading bay, the crates. "The facility has been operational since year 22. Twenty-five years of equipment. Most of it monitoring infrastructure — field units, relay nodes, the signal processing hardware." She paused. "The signal processing hardware is the bulk of it. Twelve crates just for the processing units. They're going to the Archive's general monitoring store. The oversight committee said the equipment itself was not implicated. Only the use."

Fay was standing near the loading bay door, looking at the crates. She had not said anything since they parked. She was reading the stenciled equipment codes on the crate faces with the expression of someone reading a language they recognize without having studied it.

"Is the facility logged in the eastern communities' administrative record?" she asked. It was directed at the woman with the clipboard.

The woman looked at her. "I don't know. We weren't given access to the communities' administrative systems. The decommission package was Archive-internal." She paused. "The communities — the eastern communities — they would have known the facility was here. It's not invisible. It's a three-story building."

"They would have known," Fay said. "But not what it was for."

The woman with the clipboard did not answer that. There was no answer that was not also a confirmation.

---

Corin walked through the facility's ground floor with the woman with the clipboard — her name was Renner, twenty-six years in Archive administrative contracts — while the other staff members continued their inventory work. The ground floor had been the operational space: the signal processing units in a row along the north wall, the monitoring terminals at workstations, the relay hardware in a separate room accessed through a reinforced door that was now propped open because everything inside was already inventoried and crated.

The monitoring terminals still had their screens on standby. The screensavers showed the Archive's administrative default — a slow rotation of the Archive's insignia on a dark background. No one had powered them down yet because the data archival procedure required the terminals to remain powered until the data transfer was complete.

The screens were the most present thing in the facility. Everything else had been packed or catalogued. The screens were still running.

"How long was this facility monitoring the eastern territory?" Corin asked.

"Twenty-five years," Renner said. "The facility was built for the cooperative's agricultural support contracts. The monitoring was the secondary function — the one that wasn't in the cooperative's public operational documentation." She paused. "I've been doing this — administrative contracts for Archive-adjacent operations — for my whole working life. I knew the classification structures existed. I didn't have access to what was inside them. I processed the contracts, I logged the equipment, I filed the compliance reports. I didn't have clearance for the program documentation." She looked at a terminal. "When the decommission package came through — when it included the oversight motion's findings — it was the first time I read what the contracts I had been processing were for."

"What did you do with that?" Corin asked.

Renner looked at him. It was a direct look — not unfriendly, but specific. "I'm decommissioning the facility correctly," she said. "That's what I can do. The equipment gets logged. The data gets transferred to the right repository. The building gets handed over to the transitional zone's infrastructure committee. I do the job the oversight process requires." She paused. "That's not a small thing. The decommission is the administrative action that closes the operation. If I do it wrong, the closure is wrong."

Corin looked at the screensavers turning in the dark. "That's not a small thing," he agreed.

---

Iris was in the loading bay when Corin returned from the ground floor walk-through. She had been there the whole time, looking at the crates. She had her documentation pad out and was writing. Not recording the facility for the expedition report — Corin recognized her handwriting through the data pad's screen from across the bay: she was writing in the registry's addendum format, the format she used when she was adding context to an existing entry rather than creating a new one.

"The 216," she said, without looking up. "Fourteen of them were employed by the cooperative at this facility in the years before the calibration. The cooperative's agricultural support contracts — seasonal labor, infrastructure maintenance. Their names are in the cooperative's public employment records, which are in the oversight motion's documentation." She finished the line she was writing. "The employment records ended in year 31. The year of the field calibration. Fourteen names stopped appearing in the cooperative's labor records after the calibration."

"Did they lose the employment because of the calibration?" Corin asked.

"The records don't say why the employment ended. The records say it ended." She closed the documentation pad. "The calibration happened in their employment territory. The calibration permanently removed their Resonance architecture. After the calibration, they were no longer employed by the cooperative that had contracted the monitoring operation that had been watching their territory for eleven years." She put the pad in her pack. "I'm adding that to the registry entries. The employment context. It was already true. It wasn't in the entry."

Pell was loading equipment cases back into the expedition vehicle. Fay was still standing near the loading bay entrance, looking at the road east. The terrain visible through the loading bay door was the beginning of the transitional zone's gradient toward the eastern territory — flatter, the soil darker, the pre-collapse road markers spaced differently than the Archive's standard.

Renner came out from the interior of the facility with the clipboard. "Safe expedition," she said. It was not a formal farewell — it was the farewell of someone who had met people who had something to do with a document that had changed the meaning of her work, and who was returning to the work.

"Safe decommission," Corin said.

The three of them — Corin, Iris, Fay — came out through the loading bay into the parking area. The afternoon had moved while they were inside. The sun was lower, the shadow of the facility's three-story face reaching further across the parking area than when they had arrived. The shadow edge crossed the borderlands vehicles, crossed the parking area's concrete, reached the road.

Lian was at the vehicle, the data pad in her hands. She was watching the screen with the quality of someone who has not stopped watching and has been accumulating data through the stop. "The signal is readable at this location," she said, without looking up. "Weaker than it will be further east. But readable." She paused. "The facility's signal processing hardware — while it was operational, it would have been generating interference in the substrate-adjacent frequency range. The monitoring infrastructure was broadcasting its own signature." She looked at the data pad. "The facility's hardware is powered down. The interference is gone. The substrate signal here is cleaner than it was when the facility was operational." She paused. "The decommission has made the signal measurably cleaner in a radius around this facility."

Pell climbed into the vehicle without commentary. He had the equipment log on his data pad. He had been logging the facility's decommission status, the equipment types in the crates, the relay hardware in its separate room. Not for any protocol reason — he was a monitoring technician and the facility's equipment was monitoring equipment and he had noted what he saw.

---

The vehicle drove east for forty minutes before anyone spoke.

The facility was behind them. The transitional zone was thinning — the pre-collapse structures that had been intermittent near the facility were now spaced to a kilometer or more apart, most of them roofless, the walls standing because concrete stood and the interiors had been reclaimed by whatever grew in the eastern territory when nobody was managing the growth. Water management infrastructure crossed the road at two points — channels cut from pre-collapse irrigation ditches, maintained, carrying water in a direction that was not toward the Archive's managed territory.

"It was just an office building," Fay said.

"Yes," Corin said.

"Twenty-five years of monitoring." She was looking at the road, both hands on the wheel. "My mother's family were in the cooperative's agricultural network. Before I was born and for the first years I was alive. They knew the facility. They didn't know what it was for."

"No," Corin said.

"They thought it was regional infrastructure support. That's what the contracts said." She adjusted her hands on the wheel. "That's not what the contracts were for."

No one said anything after that for another twenty minutes. The road was good enough — maintained, in the eastern communities' pattern, with the specific quality of infrastructure that had been worked on with consistent attention rather than built in a single push and then left. The water channels ran alongside the road for a kilometer before cutting north. The territory was attended to. That was visible from the vehicle.

Lian had been watching the portable array unit's screen since they left the facility.

"Signal is stronger," she said. "Stronger than it was at the facility. Stronger than it was at camp this morning." She paused. "Stronger than anything the fixed arrays have measured at any equivalent distance from the Citadel." She looked at the screen. "The facility was at the boundary of the managed territory. We're past the boundary now." She looked at the data. "The signal is responding to the territory. As we move away from the managed zones, it strengthens." She paused again. "The rhythmic variation — the rate that matches 1947 — it's clearer at this distance. Less noise from the fixed arrays, less interference from the Reckoner network. The pure signal." She looked at the data pad. "The entity is doing something specific. In this territory. In the territory where the monitoring operation spent twenty-five years watching and the calibration spent one event removing what couldn't be replaced."

The road ahead curved east, then straightened toward the late afternoon sun. The expedition vehicle drove toward it. The facility was twenty kilometers behind them and receding. The coordinate from Aldric's field notes was three days ahead.

The monitoring screen showed the rhythmic signal, steady and growing. The rate had not changed since Lian first resolved it at the monitoring boundary. It was the same rate. The same interval. The same frequency the entity had used to receive the 47.2-omega in 1947, when it understood the healing was real and began to respond.

It had been doing this for some time. That was becoming clear.

chapters.title: Chapter 3: Night Reading
chapters.text:
The camp was in a hollow between two pre-collapse earthwork berms — drainage infrastructure, from the look of them, built to redirect runoff from the transitional zone's higher ground toward the water channels that ran south and east. The berms were intact. The channels were intact. Someone had maintained them. The maintenance was not new — it had the quality of regular attention, of a specific schedule applied to a specific piece of infrastructure for years, not the recent emergency repair that temporary habitation produced.

Pell set up the monitoring array units at 1842, before the light failed. Three units in a triangle formation, twenty meters apart, all oriented east. The portable substrate array was designed for field deployment without infrastructure support — the units ran on stored charge, communicated with each other through a short-range frequency, and logged to the data pad that Lian carried. The triangle formation was the standard protocol for localized signal analysis: three readings at twenty-meter separation allowed Lian to calculate not just the signal's strength but its directionality, the angle from which it was arriving.

"I want to run continuous through the night," Lian said. She was talking to Pell, but she said it loud enough that the whole team heard. "The signal has been strengthening as we've moved east. I want to know if it continues to strengthen overnight, or if it stabilizes. I want the full curve."

"That will drain one unit's charge," Pell said.

"Switch units at 0200. We have the spares."

Pell nodded and went to finish the setup.

Iris had laid out the camp's supply distribution on a groundsheet — the food, the water, the documentation materials, the medical kit — with the systematic attention she brought to any physical inventory. She was checking items against a printed manifest. She did this without being asked, the way she did most things: because the record needed to be current, and the current version of the record was in her hands.

Fay had walked the perimeter of the hollow. She came back and sat down near the fire ring they had assembled from loose stones. She did not help with the setup — not because she wasn't willing, but because she was watching the perimeter with a quality of attention that was more useful than another pair of hands on a tent stake. The unmonitored territory had no Reckoner network, no credential readers, no surveillance infrastructure of any kind. The absence was total. Fay was not nervous about that absence — she was calibrating to it, the way a person recalibrates to a different pressure when they move to altitude. Twelve years in the Citadel's perimeter security, surrounded by sensors and coverage maps and incident response protocols, and now the hollow and the berms and nothing beyond them but the territory the Archive's monitoring had never reached.

Corin put up the first tent. He had not put up a field tent in eleven years — the last time had been a monitoring visit in the western territories, a three-day assessment of the substrate signal near the coast, which had been the closest thing to field work the Archive had asked of him in the years after the 17-X classification had restructured his role. His hands remembered the procedure even while his conscious mind was checking against it. The tent stakes went into soil that was harder than the western territory's coastal ground — the eastern transitional zone's soil was thinner, the substrate closer to the surface here than in the managed territories. He could feel it in the resistance of the stake against the hammer. The rock close below.

The fire was running by 2010. The array units' lights were green — all three units active, logging to Lian's data pad. The light from the units was a different quality than firelight — small and blue-white and steady, the specific steadiness of equipment doing its work without variation. The fire was orange and variable. The fire and the equipment lights occupied the hollow together, the two kinds of light touching the same faces.

---

Lian had been watching the data pad since the units went active. She was cross-legged next to her gear, the data pad on her knee, the screen showing three simultaneous feeds. Corin brought her food at 2030 and she ate without looking up from the screen.

At 2105 she looked up.

"Come here," she said.

The whole team came. Pell was already crouching next to her — he had been monitoring over her shoulder intermittently since the units deployed. Fay came from her position near the berm. Iris put down the document she had been reviewing and came across the hollow.

Lian showed them the screen.

Three feeds. Three signal readouts. The rhythmic variation was present in all three — the rate they had been tracking since the monitoring boundary, the rate that matched 1947. But the three feeds were not identical. They were similar. They were not the same.

"The signal is not the same at all three units," Lian said. "The variation is directional. The units are picking up the signal from different angles because the signal is coming from a specific direction. Not from the general territory around us. From a specific point." She touched the screen, indicating the angle differential between unit one and unit three. "If I calculate the directionality from the angle differential — I need to run the full calculation, but the preliminary reading shows the signal is strongest in the direction of approximately — " she paused, consulting the coordinate data she had pulled up in a side panel — "east-northeast, at a range I can't yet determine. But the direction is specific."

She looked at Corin.

"Show me the second thread's field notes," she said.

He had the notes on his data pad — the seventeen pages, scanned from Aldric's handwritten originals when the archive opened. He handed the pad to Lian. She pulled up the coordinate data from the notes, the specific location Aldric had documented in his third year of monitoring.

She compared it to the angle differential.

"East-northeast," she said. "The coordinate in the field notes is east-northeast of our current position." She looked at Corin. "The signal is pointing at the coordinate your father documented fifteen years ago."

Pell was looking at the data. "That's not within my measurement error," he said quietly. "The angle differential is clean."

"No," Lian agreed. "It's not measurement error."

---

She read Aldric's field notes aloud. This was not in any expedition protocol — it was not a technical procedure, it was not a monitoring step. It was Lian looking at the seventeen pages and understanding that the only way to convey what they said, here, in the field, with the array units running and the fire burning and the data on the screen, was to read them aloud.

She started with the field notes' header — Aldric's notation system, the date codes, the equipment specifications he had used. Then the first year's observations: the entity's substrate signal in the uncontacted territories, the variation he had noticed that differed from the managed territory's signal, the specific frequencies he had been tracking, the way the signal responded to the terrain.

She read for forty minutes. The fire burned down to coals and Fay added a piece of salvage wood without breaking the group's attention.

The first two years were a record of the monitoring's development — Aldric learning the equipment he had built from pre-collapse components, learning the variation patterns, establishing the baseline for the comparison that the third year would make possible. He had been meticulous. Every site had a notation, a time stamp, a equipment specification note. The frequency variations were logged in a table format that Aldric had designed himself because the Archive's monitoring protocols did not extend to the kind of field work he was doing in the second thread. The second thread had no institutional protocol. The protocol was Aldric.

The fire's wood settled and Fay added another piece. The hollow was warm from the fire and cold from the night in equal parts. No wind. The berms held the air still.

The third year's notes were the ones with the coordinate. Lian read them with particular slowness.

*Day 847, Site 3 (eastern coordinate, extraction zone, pre-calibration). Substrate signal at this site is different from all other monitoring points in the eastern territory. The difference is not in the signal's strength or its base frequency — both are consistent with the anomaly I have been tracking across the second thread's full monitoring range. The difference is in the structure of the variation. At every other site, the variation has the quality of diffusion — the way the entity's signal spreads across the substrate without directionality. At Site 3, the variation is organized. I don't have the vocabulary for this in my current measurement framework. The organization is not mechanical — it doesn't look like the Archive's signal architecture, it doesn't look like the cooperative's relay infrastructure. It looks like the entity is paying attention to this specific location. I cannot characterize the attention. I cannot say what the entity is attending to. I can only say: the signal at this site is doing something the signal at every other site is not doing. I have come back to this site six times. The difference is consistent. The difference is real. The investigation of what the difference means requires equipment and institutional support I cannot maintain from my current position.*

Lian looked up from the data pad.

"He stopped there," she said. "That's the last field note for Site 3. The classification came two months after that entry. He didn't get to come back."

The fire was very quiet. The array units' lights were green in the dark at the edges of the hollow. The data pad's screen showed the angle differential building — unit one and unit three, their readings diverging in the same direction, the same angle, accumulating hours of it now, the signal arriving from the same bearing it had been arriving from since the units went active. The preliminary directionality was holding. The calculation was not done. The preliminary was holding.

Corin looked at the field notes on his data pad — the image of the handwritten page, Aldric's notation, the date code for day 847. He had read the second thread's field notes four times since the archive opened. He had read them for content each time. This was the first time he had heard them read aloud in the territory they described, in the dark, with the monitoring equipment doing in real time what his father's equipment had done fifteen years ago.

He did not have a word for what that was. He had the data pad and the field notes and the fire.

Iris had been writing during the reading. She had not stopped. She looked at what she had written, which was not notes — she had been documenting the observation in the registry's addendum format, the second-thread data cross-referenced against the current expedition's monitoring output.

"The correlation is exact," she said. "Not approximately exact. Exactly exact. The coordinate Aldric documented in year 47 post-Registry is the same coordinate Lian's angle differential is pointing to tonight, in year 56." She looked at Lian. "The entity was doing this fifteen years ago. At the same location. Aldric found it and then lost access to it."

"Yes," Lian said.

---

Fay spoke after a long silence.

"The settlement nearest to that coordinate," she said. She was looking at the fire, not at the data. "The eastern communities that formed after the field calibration — the first settlements were built in the extraction zone's northern edge. That's where the people who survived the calibration went. Toward the water infrastructure they had been building." She paused. "My family was part of the second settlement wave. My grandmother was in the calibration zone. She survived it but she lost — " Fay paused. "She lost the Resonance architecture. She was one of the 216." She looked at the fire. "She was alive when I left. She was alive as of the last relay message I got from my uncle, three months ago. She'll be in the settlement."

No one said anything. The statement was not a request for anything, was not an announcement of anything. It was Fay placing the fact into the group's shared knowledge, the way she placed herself at the perimeter: because it was better for everyone if the information was in the record.

Iris looked at her. "She's in the registry," Iris said. "I'll know the entry."

Fay nodded. That was not gratitude — it was confirmation. The entry was the confirmation.

"The territory has changed since I left," Fay said. "I've been watching it since this morning. The water management — they extended the irrigation network into the eastern basin. When I left, the eastern basin was dry in summer. They solved it. Twelve years and they solved a problem the cooperative's infrastructure program said was unsolvable without Archive-level resources." She paused. "They didn't need Archive-level resources. They needed to not have their territory monitored by the thing that was contracting their labor for twenty-five years without telling them why." She looked at the berm. "They built things. While the monitoring was running, while the cooperative was contracting them, while the 17-X classification was keeping the program documentation sealed — they were building their own infrastructure. Because they needed it."

"That's what the maps don't show," Lian said. "The Archive's territory maps were built from monitoring data. The monitoring was looking for the substrate signal. It wasn't mapping the communities' construction projects."

"No," Fay said.

She stood and went back to the perimeter position she had taken at camp setup. The conversation was done — not by dismissal, but by completion. The information had been given. She was back to watching.

---

Lian logged the night's second observation at 0312, when the angle differential data had accumulated enough points for a clean directional calculation.

She wrote: *Second observation. The substrate signal in the unmonitored territory is directional. Triangulated angle from three portable array units at camp position 2, night 2 of expedition. Signal strongest in direction of coordinate matching Aldric Draven second thread field notes site 3. Correlation precision: within the margin of field measurement. Consistency check against fixed array anomaly map: directional finding is consistent with the macro-level anomaly distribution, which is stronger in the eastern community territory than anywhere else in the unrestored zone.*

She paused at the final line. She wrote: *The signal is a vector, not a field. It has a source.*

She sent the log to the expedition's documentation record. She sent a duplicate to Iris, who was still awake across the hollow, still writing.

The array units ran through the night. The signal was consistent. The rate was the same rate it had been since the monitoring boundary: the 1947 rate, the healing response rate, the rate the entity had demonstrated when it understood for the first time in centuries that something was being offered that was not extraction.

Lian had been watching the substrate signal for twenty years. She had watched it through the fixed arrays, through the portable equipment during field work, through the monitoring records that stretched back to the Archive's founding and the deep archive data that stretched further than that. She knew the signal's signatures the way someone knows a voice — not by analysis but by exposure. By the accumulated hours of attention.

The signal tonight was not the signal she had been watching for twenty years in the managed territories.

It was the same entity. It was the same substrate. The frequency range was the same. But the variation — the rhythmic variation at the 1947 rate — had a character here that the fixed arrays had been too coarse to show. The variation was not noise on top of a signal. It was the signal's structure. The signal was organized around the variation the way a sentence is organized around its syntax. The variation was not happening to the signal. The variation was the signal.

She would not put that in the observation log. The observation log was for measurement data. The interpretation of measurement data was a different document. But she was thinking it, in the hollow, in the small blue light of the data pad, with the two other units' lights steady at the triangle's other corners.

The coordinate was two days east.

Lian watched the screen and let the units run.

chapters.title: Chapter 4: Arrival
chapters.text:
The outer settlement's first visible structure was a water tower — pre-collapse steel frame, new polymer tank at the top, mounted on a hillside east of the road at a height that would service the settlement's pressure infrastructure. The tower was painted. The paint was recent enough that the color was still even, a dark grey that absorbed the midday sun without reflecting it. The tower had the Archive's standard elevation markings on its frame — pre-collapse notation, the same system the Archive had inherited and never replaced — but the paint over the markings was the settlement's own, and the markings underneath had been used as a grid reference for the pressure calculation, not as Archive documentation.

"They painted over the frame markings," Pell said. He was in the back seat, looking at the tower as the vehicle slowed.

"They used them first," Fay said. "The elevation markings — those are useful. They used them to calibrate the tank pressure and then painted them into the structure. So they're permanent." She was watching the road, not the tower. "That's how building works out here. You use what's here. You don't throw it out because of where it came from."

The settlement itself was behind the tower, at the base of the hill: a cluster of structures running from the pre-collapse concrete buildings along the main axis to newer construction in polymer-and-salvage on the settlement's north and east perimeter. The newer construction used the pre-collapse buildings as anchor points, as if the settlement had grown outward from the concrete cores over decades. Which it had. The expansion was legible in the materials — older concrete, newer polymer, very new wood framing at the outermost edges, wood that had been milled locally, the cut marks still fresh.

The road into the settlement had a gate — not a security gate, a junction marker, a post with reflective tape at the intersection of the main road and the settlement's internal road system. Someone was standing at the gate.

She was approximately fifty. She was wearing the eastern communities' standard work clothing — sturdy, practical, the kind of garment that was designed to be repaired rather than replaced. She was standing at the gate in the way of someone who had been notified that a vehicle was coming and had come to meet it, not in the way of someone who had been posted there to stop traffic.

Fay stopped the vehicle at the gate.

The woman looked at the vehicle. She looked at the five people in it. Her expression did not change.

"Expedition team from the Archive's liaison office," Fay said through the window. "Field monitoring work. We sent notice through the Timelost relay three days ago."

"I received the notice," the woman said. "I'm Maret. The council representative for this settlement." She paused. "Come in."

---

The administrative building was on the main axis, twenty meters from the gate, in one of the pre-collapse concrete buildings. The building had been retrofitted — interior walls added, the ceiling repaired, the windows replaced with polymer-framed glass that was locally made, slightly inconsistent in thickness, catching the light in a way that the Archive's standard construction did not. The floors were concrete, sealed with a material that had the color and texture of the eastern communities' standard sealer, which Corin had seen documented in the oversight committee's infrastructure reports but had never seen in use.

The walls in the main room of the administrative building had maps on them. Hand-drawn maps on large sheets of pre-collapse paper, the lines made with ink that had faded at different rates, the oldest maps' annotations in a darker hand than the newest additions. The maps showed the water infrastructure: channels, tanks, pressure lines, the locations of the wells and the pumping stations. There were eight maps total, covering the settlement's full water system from the main intake in the hills to the distribution lines at the settlement's perimeter.

Maret stood in front of the central map — the main system overview — and looked at the expedition team as they came in. She looked at Corin. She looked at Iris. She looked at Fay with a particular quality, a different quality than she looked at the others, a recognition that was not social but structural — she knew who Fay was, or knew what Fay's presence meant.

"You're the first Archive people here since the calibration," she said.

She said it without inflection. That was what made it specific. It was a fact. A fact that was also a history. A fact that carried thirty-one years of content in its phrasing — not because she was loading it with that content, but because the fact contained it.

"Yes," Corin said.

Maret looked at the central map. "The censure motion. We received the findings through the Timelost relay. The oversight committee's documentation — the findings on what the 17-X program authorized, the findings on the field calibration's classification, the motion to void the contracts and decommission the Section 7 facilities." She paused. "We read all of it. There are eleven people in this settlement who were in the field calibration zone. They read it too."

"Fourteen," Iris said.

Maret looked at her.

"The registry documents fourteen 216 entries in this settlement. Eleven who were in the calibration zone directly. Three who were in the adjacent territory and experienced secondary effects." Iris had the documentation pad. "The secondary-effect category was added after the Congregation's testimony — it's a newer category. The three entries may not have been publicly visible in the documentation you received through the relay."

Maret looked at her for a long moment. "Fourteen," she said.

"Yes."

Maret was quiet. She looked at the water infrastructure map. The map showed the full extent of what the settlement had built in thirty-one years — the main channels in the oldest ink, the pressure system in a later hand, the eastern basin extension in the newest lines, the ink still fresh enough to be slightly raised from the paper surface.

"You said the expedition is field monitoring work," she said. "The substrate signal anomaly. The relay notice described it."

"The anomaly signal is strongest in this territory," Corin said. "We're here to take direct field readings at a specific coordinate. The coordinate corresponds to documentation in the Archive's second-thread research — my father's field monitoring work from fifteen years ago." He paused. "The coordinate is in the extraction zone where the field calibration happened."

Maret looked at him. She was processing this — the liaison's name, the second thread, the connection to Aldric Draven's research, the coordinate.

"Your father's research," she said.

"His field notes documented something at that coordinate that the classification prevented him from investigating." Corin looked at the water map. "The entity's signal is doing something at that location. We need to read it directly."

Maret was quiet for a long time.

"The extraction zone," she said. "Where the calibration happened. The entity's signal is strongest there." She said this not as a question but as a fact she was placing next to the other facts. She looked at the water infrastructure map. The eastern basin extension ran through the edge of what had been the extraction zone's northern boundary. The settlement had been building infrastructure in the same territory where the calibration had stripped the architecture from eleven people — fourteen — and the entity was doing something specific in the substrate of that ground.

"Yes," Corin said.

Maret was quiet for another moment. She looked at the map, at the eastern basin extension's newest ink line, at the point where it crossed into the extraction zone. She had been living in proximity to that ground for thirty-one years. She had drawn some of these map lines herself. She had supervised the digging of the channel that ran through the extraction zone's northern edge. She knew that ground.

"When you take the field readings," she said. "What does the result tell you? What does the institution plan to do with the finding?"

"We don't know the result yet," Corin said. "The finding will be reported to the oversight committee and the monitoring committee. What the institution does next depends on what the data shows."

"The institution has made plans in this territory before," Maret said. "The cooperative's contract was a plan. The field calibration was a plan." She was not accusing him of anything — the statement had the quality of Maret placing a fact into the room, the same way she had placed the arrival fact. "I want to know whether this expedition is the beginning of another plan that the settlement hears about afterward."

Corin looked at her. The question was fair. The question was more than fair — it was the question that the liaison position existed to answer differently than the Archive had answered it before.

"The expedition's findings go to the oversight committee," he said. "The oversight committee's process is public — the findings documentation goes to the Timelost relay. You'll have the same access to the findings as anyone else in the institution." He paused. "The next decision — what the institution does with the finding — will not be made without the settlement being informed first. That is not the oversight committee's existing protocol. That is what I will recommend as the liaison protocol going forward."

Maret looked at him. She was measuring that against the thirty-one years and the cooperative's contracts and the Timelost relay access and the decommission notice that had arrived four weeks ago for a facility that had been monitoring her community's substrate for twenty-five years without her knowledge.

"All right," she said. Not agreement. Acknowledgment. The distinction was legible in her face.

---

Iris asked about the registry at the end of the meeting.

She asked it the way she asked most direct questions — without preamble, after the operational information had been established. "I have physical copies of the 216 registry entries. I'd like to offer them to the people whose names are in the registry, if they want to receive them. I won't bring the registry to people who don't want it."

Maret looked at her. The look was not hostile and it was not simple. It was the look of someone calculating what the offer meant — not for herself, but for the people she was responsible to.

"The entries — what do they say?" Maret asked.

"Name, date of harm, category of harm, status. The status says: 'Not eligible for restoration. Record includes this person.' I can tell you what 'record includes this person' means."

"Tell me."

"It means the Archive's official documentation — permanently — contains this person's harm in a column that cannot be closed. It cannot be resolved as restored, because the harm cannot be undone. It cannot be removed, because it happened. The entry stays open, in the record, as a permanent acknowledgment that the harm occurred and that the institution knows it occurred." Iris paused. "The registry was created because the institution had a category for harm that it had resolved and no category for harm it had done that it could not resolve. The 216 entries created the second category."

Maret was looking at the water map. Her hand was not on the table — she had her arms crossed, the posture of someone thinking through a specific problem with her whole body.

"The people whose names are in it," she said. "Some of them may not want to see their name in an Archive document. Even a document that says what this one says." She looked at Iris. "The Archive's documents have meant specific things in this territory. The cooperative's contracts. The monitoring records that people didn't know existed. An entry in an Archive registry — even one that says 'record includes this person' — is still an Archive document."

"Yes," Iris said. "That's why I'm asking you to ask them."

Maret looked at her. She uncrossed her arms. "I'll go ask."

She left through the door at the back of the administrative building. Her footsteps receded into the settlement's sound — voices, the creak of a door, the distant sound of the water infrastructure's pressure system working.

---

Corin stood at the water infrastructure map. He traced the easternmost channel line with his finger, not touching the paper, following the ink's path from the main system toward the eastern basin. The line went through the territory where the calibration had happened. The settlement had run water through that ground and the water was feeding the basin and the basin was green in a way the pre-collapse documentation said it hadn't been in decades.

The maps were a record of thirty-one years of specific decisions — where to dig, where to route the water, how to manage the pressure across the settlement's growth. The oldest map had the handwriting of whoever had drawn it in the first years after the calibration, working from pre-collapse reference materials and local knowledge of the terrain. The newest map extension had different handwriting, younger, the line weights more precise — someone who had learned drafting from the settlement's own technical education rather than from pre-collapse training materials. The knowledge had been transmitted. The infrastructure had been transmitted. The settlement had been doing this — transmitting, building, extending, maintaining — for the entire period that the Archive had classified the calibration and sealed the second thread and let the Section 7 facility run its twenty-five years of monitoring.

Fay was at the window, looking into the settlement. She had recognized people. Corin had seen it in the quality of her attention — the specific focus that came from seeing a face that matched a name from twelve years ago. She had not spoken to anyone. She was not ready to speak to anyone. She was watching. Her uncle's relay messages described the settlement in words — the new water tower, the eastern basin, the school building they had started three years ago. The relay messages were not photographs. They were not this window, this view, these specific people moving through the settlement's paths in the midday light.

Lian was outside with Pell, running a spot-check reading with one of the portable array units. The settlement was not at the coordinate — the coordinate was eight kilometers east — but the signal would be readable here, and the reading would add a data point to the directionality calculation. From outside the window, Corin could see Lian crouching next to the unit's display, her hand blocking the sun from the screen. Pell was writing something in the equipment log. The settlement's people who were moving through the paths gave the two of them wide berth — not hostility, just the specific distance that the Archive's visible equipment produced in a community that had been monitored without consent for twenty-five years.

Iris was at the table. She had the registry's printed copy in her hands. She was looking at it. The printed copy was eighty-three pages — the full 216 entries, the category documentation, the addendum records she had added since the Congregation's testimony. She was not reading it. She was holding it. The paper had the weight of what was in it — not physical weight, the weight of the specific words on each page, the weight of Iris having written each entry in the format she had developed because the existing format did not have a category for this.

The administrative building was quiet. The sound of Maret's conversation somewhere in the settlement was inaudible now. There were only the maps on the wall, the water infrastructure annotated in layers of ink, and Iris at the table with the registry in her hands.

She waited.

chapters.title: Chapter 5: The Registry
chapters.text:
Maret returned with seven people.

She brought them through the door at the back of the administrative building — the door that opened into the settlement's interior paths — and they came in without ceremony, without announcement, in the specific way of people who had decided to come and were now executing the decision. They arranged themselves without instruction along the administrative building's interior wall and the table's edges. They did not sit in the formal arrangement of a meeting. They stood in the arrangement of people who had not decided yet how long they would stay.

Iris counted them. Seven. Maret had said fourteen people in the settlement. Not all of them had wanted to come. Seven had.

The ages varied. Corin looked at the faces and tried not to reconstruct the entries from them, because the entries had not been written to describe faces. The oldest person in the room was a woman who looked sixty or a few years past. She was standing near the door, with the quality of someone who had come because they had already carried this for thirty-one years and coming was not a burden on top of that, it was a continuation. The youngest looked thirty or slightly younger — which meant they had been an infant or a very small child at the calibration. They would not remember the calibration. They would have been told about it.

One of the seven was a man who looked forty-five, who had positioned himself near the center of the group, not close to either wall. He was watching Iris with the quality of someone who had thought carefully about what they wanted from this encounter and had not finished the calculation.

Iris set the registry's printed copy on the table.

"I want to read each of you your own entry," she said. "You don't have to accept the physical copy. You don't have to respond. I'll read what the registry says about you, and then I'll tell you what the entry means institutionally. That's what I came to do."

No one objected. The seven people watched her.

She opened to the section with the settlement's entries — she had organized the printed copy by settlement before departure, which meant the fourteen entries for this settlement were consecutive, separated from the broader 216 by tabbed dividers she had made from folded paper. She read the first entry. Name, date of harm, category: Resonance Architecture Removal (Field Calibration, Eastern Zone, Year 31 Pre-Registry). Status: Not eligible for restoration. Record includes this person.

She looked up.

"'Record includes this person' means the Archive's official documentation contains your harm in a column that cannot be closed. It cannot be marked as resolved, because the harm cannot be undone. It cannot be removed from the registry, because it happened and the registry's function is to contain the complete record of what happened. This entry is permanent. It will be in the registry as long as the registry exists. It will be in every version of the registry that follows the current version." She paused. "The purpose of the entry is to prevent the institution from forgetting that this happened to you. Not to resolve what happened. To make it permanently part of the record."

She read the second entry. The woman it belonged to looked at the table surface. Her expression did not change. She took the printed page when Iris offered it, folded it in half, and put it in her jacket pocket.

The third entry belonged to the youngest person in the room — thirty or slightly younger, who had been a child at the calibration. The entry's date of harm was the calibration date. The category was the same as the others. The status was the same as the others: *Not eligible for restoration. Record includes this person.* Iris read it aloud the same way she had read the others. The young person was listening with the quality of someone who has been told about a thing their entire life and is now hearing the official language for it. The official language was not the thing. The official language was the institution's acknowledgment that the thing happened. Those were two different documents.

They took the printed page. They folded it without looking at it and put it in their pocket.

Iris read five more entries. Two people took their printed pages. One person said nothing, received the page, looked at it for a long time, and gave it back. Iris did not put it back in the stack — she set it on the table between them, and left it there.

---

The man who had positioned himself near the center of the group waited until the sixth entry.

He had his arms at his sides — not crossed, not in his pockets, not the posture of resistance or comfort. He had the posture of someone standing in an open field, visible from all directions.

Iris read his entry. Name: Bern. Date of harm: Year 31 Pre-Registry. Category: Resonance Architecture Removal (Field Calibration, Eastern Zone, Year 31 Pre-Registry). Status: Not eligible for restoration. Record includes this person.

She looked at him.

"I was fourteen," he said. "I was in the extraction zone with my father. We were doing seasonal labor for the cooperative's agricultural contracts. The calibration — we didn't know what it was. We knew something had happened to us. We didn't have a name for it for years." He paused. "The cooperative's contract documentation didn't have a category for it either."

"No," Iris said.

He looked at the registry entry. "Why now?" he asked. "The censure was this year. The testimony was this year. This is being done because the institutional process reached this point." He was not accusing her of anything. He was asking a question that had a structure to it — a question about chains of causation, about what had to be true before this could happen.

Iris looked at him.

"The testimony," she said. "The censure. The declassification that opened the 17-X archive. The oversight committee's jurisdiction over the voided contracts. The liaison position — Corin's position, which was created after the oversight motion passed. The Congregation's records, which gave us the 216 names in the form that could be entered into the registry." She paused. "The chain of things that had to happen before the institutional capacity to make this registry existed." She looked at the entry. "The entry could not have existed in the form it exists before those things happened. The category did not exist. The Congregation was not in dialogue with the Archive. The second thread was classified. The cooperative structure was operational." She paused. "All of those things resolved in a specific sequence, and the sequence ended with me being able to create the category and put your name in it."

He looked at her. "Thirty-one years," he said.

"Yes," she said.

He looked at the printed entry in her hands, not at her. He was not done with the calculation.

---

Iris told him about the documentation.

She did not plan to. She had come with the registry, the entries, the procedural explanation of what the entries meant. She had not come to tell anyone about her own documentation practice. The expedition protocol did not include her documentation practice.

She told him because the calculation in his face required an honest answer, and the honest answer was not only the chain of institutional events. The honest answer included what she had been doing before the institutional chain completed.

"I had some of the 216 names in my personal documentation before the Congregation gave me the formal list," she said. "Not all of them. Twenty-three names. I had encountered those names in my registry work over the years — in older damage assessment records, in the cooperative's labor documentation that was publicly filed, in records from the immediate post-calibration period that were not classified." She paused. "The records existed. The names were in them. They described harm that the Archive had done and that the existing registry had no category for." She was looking at the entry. "I began filing documentation for those twenty-three names in a personal registry supplement. A document that was not official. A document that had no institutional backing, no official category, no authorization to exist as an Archive record." She looked at Bern. "I was filing the registry entries before I had institutional authorization to file them. I had been carrying this — those twenty-three names, the documentation of the harm they had experienced — in a document outside the official record because the official record didn't have a category for it yet."

The administrative building was quiet. The seven people along the wall were watching her.

"How long?" Bern asked.

"Eleven years. The cooperative's labor documentation that contained the first names I could identify — that was available in year 45. I started the personal supplement in year 46." She paused. "When the Congregation provided the full 216 list, I found twenty-three names I recognized from my personal supplement. I had been documenting those twenty-three people for nine years without the institution knowing the documentation existed."

Bern looked at her. His face changed. The calculation in his face did not resolve — it didn't finish and arrive at a result. But the quality of what he was calculating changed. He was no longer calculating only the chain of institutional events. He was calculating something about the woman standing in front of him.

"Why didn't you bring that to the institution?" he asked.

"I tried," she said. "In year 47 and in year 49. I submitted requests to create a new harm category for the cooperative's field calibration victims. Both requests were returned without action — the first because the field calibration was in the 17-X classified scope and the reviewer couldn't examine the underlying documentation, the second because the institutional calendar was occupied with the restoration deployment preparation." She paused. "I filed the requests through the correct channel and I received the correct institutional response. The correct institutional response was: not yet. So I kept the personal documentation until the institutional channel opened."

The woman near the door — the oldest, who had come because coming was a continuation of what she had been doing — was watching Iris with a quality that was different from the seven people's initial watching. The initial watching had been assessment. This was something else.

"You recognized my name," Bern said. It was not a question.

"Yes," Iris said. "Your name was in the cooperative's labor records from year 31. You were listed as a minor — fourteen years old — on your father's seasonal contract. I found the record in year 46. Your name was in my personal supplement from year 46 to year 54." She paused. "Eight years."

He looked at her. He looked at the printed entry.

He did not say whether it was enough. He did not say it was not enough. Both of those statements were available to him and he did not choose either. He took the printed entry from Iris's hands and looked at it for a long time.

Then he folded it and put it in the chest pocket of his jacket.

---

Maret read the last entry herself — she was not one of the 216, but she had held the meeting's structure, and reading the last entry was her way of closing it. She read it aloud the same way Iris had read the others. The person it belonged to was not in the room — they had not come. Maret read it as a statement of record, into the room, with the people who were present.

When she finished, there was a long silence.

The seven people arranged along the wall and the table edge began, one by one, to move toward the door. Not in a group — in the individual movements of people who had each arrived at a different conclusion and were each leaving on their own terms. Some had their printed pages. Some had left them on the table. The woman near the door went last. She paused at the threshold.

"The entity," she said. She was looking at Corin, not at Iris. "It's doing something in the extraction zone. You said its signal is strongest there."

"Yes," Corin said.

"We felt nothing," she said. "When the deployment happened — the 47.2-omega, the restoration beginning — we felt nothing. The people who had lost their Resonance architecture felt nothing. The entity's signal went through us and there was nothing to receive it." She paused. "And now the entity is doing something in the ground where it happened."

"Yes," Corin said.

She looked at him for a moment. Her face was the face of sixty-two years of carrying a fact about her own body that the institution had classified for twenty-six of those years and had not acknowledged for the remaining five until the Congregation's testimony reached the oversight committee. She had the quality of someone who has been patient for so long that patience has become a structural element of her character rather than a response to circumstance.

"I want to know what it's doing," she said. It was not a request. It was a statement of something she would continue to want regardless of what Corin did about it.

"So do I," Corin said.

She left.

Maret came to the door after the seven people had gone. She stood in the frame and looked at the room — the table, the returned pages on it, Iris, Corin, the administrative building's quiet.

"There are three more in the settlement who didn't come," Maret said. "Not because they didn't want to. Two are in the medical facility — not serious conditions, but the facility is across the settlement. One is on the water infrastructure maintenance rotation for the eastern channel today. He knew you were here. He sent me to say: he'll take his copy of the entry when you're available."

Iris looked at Maret. "Now," she said.

Maret took her to the maintenance person at the eastern channel. He was standing in the channel bed, up to his knees in the water flow, working on a pressure valve that had been running low since the last maintenance cycle. He took the printed page from Iris with wet hands, read it once, folded it into the breast pocket of his work jacket where it would get wet and he didn't care, and went back to the valve.
